2 Class 1: 2023-08-14 2.1 Sets Properties of a set: order doesn’t matter redundancies don’t matter \(X=Y\) means (by def) \(x\in X\) iff \(x \in Y\) 2.1.1 Subsets \(X \subseteq Y\) if for each \(x\in X, x \in Y\). Remark 1 If \(X \subseteq Y\) and it is not the case that \(Y \subseteq X\) then we say \(X\) is a proper subset of \(Y\). We denote this as \(X \subsetneq Y\). Remark 2 For any set \(X, \emptyset \subseteq X\) Important Object: The set of all subsets of \(X = 2^X\) Example: if \(X = \{a, b, c\}\) then \(2^X = \{\emptyset, \{a\}, \{b\}, \{c\}, \{a,b\}, ..., \{a,b,c\}\}\) 2.1.2 Operations on Sets Union \(X \cup Y = \{x: x \in X 	ext{ or } x \in Y\}\) (or for an arbitrary number of sets: \(\cup_{i\in I} X_i=\{x:x\in X_i 	ext{ for some } i \in I\}\)) Intersection \(U \cap Y = \{x: x \in X 	ext{ and } x \in Y\}\) (or for an arbitrary number of sets: \(\cap_{i\in I} X_i=\{x:x\in X_i 	ext{ for all } i \in I\}\)) Difference: \(Y \setminus X = \{x \in Y: x 
ot\in X\}\) \(E \subseteq X\): Complement of \(E\): \(E^C = X\setminus E\) Say \(X\) and \(Y\) are disjoint if \(X \cap Y=\emptyset\). Say \((X_i: i \in I))\) are disjoint if \(\cap X_{i\in I} = \emptyset\) Say pairwise disjoint if for each \(i
eq j, X_i \cap X_j = \emptyset\). Caution: \(X_1 = \{0,1,2\}, X_1 = \{2,3\}, X_1 = \{3,0\}\) are disjoint, but not pairwise disjoint. Partition of \(X\): \(P \subseteq 2^x\) is a partition of \(X\) if the following hold: if \(E, F \in P\) and \(E\cap F
eq \emptyset\) then \(E=F\) \(U_{E_u\in P}E_i=X\) Cartesian Product: \((X_i: i\in I)\) \(\Pi_{i \in I} X_i = \{(x_i: i\in I): x_i \in X_i\}\) Caution: never use curly braces when working with a Cartesian product. Order matters in a Cartesian product. If \((X_i: i = 1, ... ,n)\) where \(X_i=X\) for each \(i=1, ..., n\), then \(X_n=\Pi_{i=1}^n X_i\). We call this the n-fold product. 2.2 Relations Relation on \(X 	imes Y: R\) : idea is that the relation, \(R\), expresses a relationship between members of \(X\) and members of \(Y\). Example: \(\geq\) is a relation on \(\mathbb{R} 	imes \mathbb{R}\) such that \(x\geq y\) iff \(x\) is greater than or equal to \(y\) \(>\) is a relation on \(\mathbb{R} 	imes \mathbb{R}\) such that \(x > y\) iff \(x\) is strictly greater than \(y\) We can view \(\geq\) as a subset of \(\mathbb{R} 	imes \mathbb{R}\). In notation: \(\{(x,y) \in \mathbb{R} 	imes \mathbb{R}: x \geq y\}\) A relation, \(R\), on \(X 	imes Y\) is some \(R \subseteq X 	imes Y, (x,y) \in R\). Can also write \(xRy\). Possible properties of a relation, \(R\), on \(X 	imes X\): Reflexive: for each \(x \in X, xRx\) Transitive: if \(xRy\) and \(yRz\) then \(xRz\). Symmetric: if \(xRy\) then \(yRx\) Antisymmetric: if \(xRy\) and \(yRx\) then \(x=y\). Complete: for each \(x, y \in X\), either \(xRy\) or \(yRx\). Example: \(\geq\) is reflexive transitive NOT symmetric antisymmetric (only way for to be greater than y and y greater than x is for them to be equal) complete (yep, can order any two numbers) Example: \(>\) is NOT reflexive transitive NOT symmetric YES antisymmetric (trivially - the sufficient condition is not satisfied (impossible for \(xRy\) and \(yRx\) to happen here), so the necessary condition is irrelevant) NOT complete (can’t order the same number) Example: \(\geq\) on \(\mathbb{R} 	imes \mathbb{R}\), such that \((x_1,x_2) \geq (y_1,y_2) \iff x_1 \geq y_1, x_2 \geq y_2\). This is reflexive transitive NOT symmetric antisymmetric NOT complete (cannot rank ordered pairs) 2.2.1 Equivalence Relation on \(X 	imes X\) is reflexive symmetric transitive Example: \(X = \{a,c,l\}\). Preference relationship on \(X 	imes X: R\) is \(aRl, lRA, aRA, lRl, cRc, aRc, lRc\). This is not an equivalence relation because it is not symmetric. But, I can derive an indifference relationship, \(I\) from \(R\): a\(I\)l, l\(I\)a, a\(I\)a, l\(I\)l, c\(I\)c. And this is an equivalence relation! 2.3 Functions \(f: X ightarrow Y\): means for any one input, exactly one output for a given output, there may be multiple inputs (ex. \(x_1, x_2, x_1 
eq x_2, f(x_1) = f(x_2) = y\)) Graph(\(f\)) = \(\{(x,y) \in X 	imes Y: y = f(x)\} \subseteq X 	imes Y\). A function is a specific relation, \(R\), on \(X 	imes Y\) that satisfies: for each \(x \in X\), there exists \(y \in Y\) such that \(xRy\). if \(xRy_1\) and \(xRy_2\) then \(y_1=y_2\). Given \(E \subseteq X\), write \(f(E) = \{y \in Y: \exists x \in E\}\) such that \(f(x)=y\). We call this the image of \(E\) under \(f\) \(f(X) \subseteq Y\) <– image of \(f\). Caution: always need \(f(E) 
eq \emptyset\) if \(E 
eq \emptyset\). Given \(E \subseteq Y: f^{-1}(E) = \{x \in X: f(x) \in E \}\). This is called the inverse image of \(E\) under \(f\). Example: Let \(h: \mathbb{R_+} ightarrow \mathbb{R}\) where \(h(x) = 1  orall x\in \mathbb{R}\) and let \(E = \{2\}\). Then, \(h^{-1}(E) = \emptyset\). Definition: Call \(f: X ightarrow Y\) invertible if for each \(y \in Y\) there exists exactly \(x\in X\) with \(f(x) = y\). An invertible function induces a function \(f^{-1}: Y ightarrow X\). In particular, think of that function as \(g: Y ightarrow X\) such that \(g(y)=x\) iff \(f^{-1}(\{y\})=\{x\}\)

3 Class 2: 2023-08-15 3.1 Real Numbers Def The function \(f: X \rightarrow Y\) is invertible if for each \(y \in Y\) there exists a unique \(x \in X\) such that \(f(x) = y\). Def Fix \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) nonempty. Call \(u \in \mathbb{R}\) an upper bound of \(X\) if, for each \(x \in X, u \geq x\) Call \(l \in \mathbb{R}\) a lower bound of \(X\) if, for each \(x \in X, x \geq l\) Def Call \(u^*\) a least upper bound (supremum) of \(X\) if \(u^*\) is an upper bound of \(X\) and, for each upper bound \(u, u \geq u^*\). Denoted \(\sup X\). Remark \(\sup X\) and \(\inf X\) need not be members of \(X\). Proof: Consider \([0,1) = X\). Then \(sup X = 1\). And \(1 \geq X\) for \(x \in X\). \(1 > u\) there exists \(1 > x > u\) and so \(u\) is not an upper bound. Completeness Axiom: Let \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) be nonempty. If \(X\) is bounded above (resp bounded below) then \(X\) has a least upper bound (resp a greatest lower bound.) Notational convention: If \(X\) nonempty and has an upper bound, then \(\sup X \in \mathbb{R}\) exists. But if \(X\) nonempty and does not have an upper bound, we write \(\sup X = \infty\). Proposition 1: Suppose \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) is nonempty and has an upper bound. Then \(\sup X\) is unique for each \(\varepsilon >0\) there exists \(x \in X\) such that \(\sup X - \varepsilon < x\). Proof fix \(u_1, u_2\) that are both least upper bounds of \(X\). Then \(u_1 \leq u_2\), since \(u_1\) is a least upper bound. Similarly, \(u_2 \leq u_1\), since \(u_2\) is a least upper bound. Hence, \(u_1=u_2\). By contradiction. Suppose not. Then there exists \(\varepsilon > 0\) such that for all \(x \in X, \sup X - \varepsilon \geq x\). Then \(\sup X - \varepsilon\) is an upper bound with \(\sup X - \varepsilon < \sup X\) contradicting \(\sup X\) being supremum. Def Fix \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) nonempty Call \(\bar{x}\) a max of \(X\) if \(\bar{x} \in X\) and \(\bar{x} \geq x\) for each \(x \in X\) Call \(\underline{x}\) a min of \(X\) if \(\underline{x} \in X\) and \(\underline{x} \leq x\) for each \(x \in X\) Example: [0,1) doesn’t have a max, even though it’s bounded Proposition 2 Fix \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) nonempty \(\bar{x}\) is a max of \(X\) iff \(\bar{x}=\sup X \in X\) \(\underline{x}\) is a max of \(X\) iff \(\underline{x}=\inf X \in X\) Pf: a) \(\bar{x} \in X\), we know this from the def of the max. b) need to show \(\bar{x}=\sup X\). Well, we know \(\bar{x}\) is an upper bound and there exists a least upper bound \(=sup X\) by the completeness axiom. Since \(\bar{x} \in X\) each upper bound of \(X\) is greater than or equal to \(\bar{x}\). Hence, \(\bar{x}=\sup X\). Corollary 1: Fix \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) nonempty. Then \(X\) has a max iff \(\sup X \in X\). Moreover, if \(X\) has a max, it is unique. A real-valued function, \(f: X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\). Call \(f\) bounded if \(f(X) \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) is bounded. 3.2 Metric Spaces Def A metric on \(X\) is a function \(d: X \times X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) such that for each \(x,y\in X, d(x,y) \geq 0\) for each \(x,y\in X, d(x,y) = 0\) iff \(x=y\) for each \(x,y\in X, d(x,y)=d(y,x)\) (symmetry) for each \(x,y,z\in X, d(x,z) \leq d(x,y) + d(y,z)\) (triangle inequality) Def A metric space is a pair \((X,d)\) where \(X\) is a nonempty set and \(d\) is a metric on \(X\). Example 1: Let \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) nonempty. The usual metric is \(d^u: X \times X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) such that \(d^u(x,y) = |x-y|\). Example 2: \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n\) nonempty. The euclidean metric \(d^{euc}: X \times X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) is \(d^{euc}(\vec{x}, \vec{y}) = \sqrt{\Sigma^n_{i=1} (x_i-y_i)^2}\) Note that when \(n=1\) we have \(d^u(x,y)=d^{euc}(x,y)\) Any time we look at a subset of \(\mathbb{R}^n\) we’re going to impose euclidean distance, unless we are explicitly told otherwise. Example 3: “taxicab” metric. \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n\) nonempty: \(d^t(\vec{x}, \vec{y}) = \sum_{i=1}^n |x_i - y_i|\) \(d^t(\vec{x}, \vec{y}) \geq d^{euc}(\vec{x}, \vec{y})\). Example 4: Fix some nonempty set, \(X\). Discrete metric: \(d^{disc} (x,y) = \{1 \text{ if } x \neq y, 0 \text{ if } x=y\) Example 5: \((X, \rho), (\mathbb{R}, d^u)\), where \(\rho: X\times X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) is a metric. \(F^B(X, \mathbb{R}) =\) set of bounded \(f: X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) \(d: F^B(X, \mathbb{R}) \times F^B(X, \mathbb{R}) \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) such that \(d(f,g) = \sup_{x\in X} d^u(f(x), g(x)) = \sup_{x\in X} |f(x) - g(x)|\) 3.3 Sequences Def Fix a metric space \((X,d)\). A sequence on \((X,d)\) is a function \(f: \mathbb{N} \rightarrow X\). Def Fix a sequence \((x_n:n\in \mathbb{N})\) on \((X,d)\). The limit of \((x_n:n\in \mathbb{N})\) is some \(x \in X\) such that for each \(\varepsilon >0\) there exists \(N\) with \(d(x_n, x)< \varepsilon\) for all \(n\geq N\). The sequence converges if it has a limit in \(X\). The sequence diverges if it does not have a limit in \(X\). Proposition 1: Any sequence has at most one limit. Proof: Fix a sequence \((x_n: n \in \mathbb{N})\) and suppose that \(x, y\) are both limits of the sequence. want to show: x = y We know there exists \(N(x)\) such that for all \(n \geq N(x), d(x_n, x) < \varepsilon / 2\) there exists \(N(y)\) such that for all \(n \geq N(y), d(x_n, y) < \varepsilon / 2\) Let \(N = \max\{N(x), N(y)\}\). For each \(n \geq N\), \(d(x,y) \geq d(x, x_n) + d(x_n,y) < \varepsilon /2 + \varepsilon / 2 = \varepsilon\). So, \(d(x,y) < \varepsilon\) for all \(\varepsilon > 0\). So, \(d(x,y)=0\). So, \(x=y\). QED.

4 Class 3: 2023-08-16 4.1 Sequences, cont. 4.1.1 Def of Limit A limit of \((x_n, n\in \mathbb{N})\) is some \(x \in X\) such that for each \(\varepsilon >0\), there exists \(N\) with \(d(x_n, x) < \varepsilon\) for all \(n \geq N\). The sequence converges if it has a limit in \(X\). Otherwise it diverges. Special case of interest: real-valued sequence (whenever \(X\) is a subset of the reals), \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}, d = d^u\) A sequence is just a mapping of the natural numbers to \(X\). (i.e., \(f: \mathbb{N} \rightarrow X\)) Example 1: \((1/n, n \geq 1)\) on \((\mathbb{R}, d^u)\). \(\lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} 1/n = 0\): Fix \(\varepsilon >0\). To show: there exists \(N\) such that \[|1/n - 0| < \varepsilon\] for all \(n \geq N\). Take \(N = 1/\varepsilon\). Rearrange to get \(1/N < \varepsilon\). And clearly, any \(n > N\) will also satisfy this inequality. So, we have proved that the limit converges to 0. Example 1b \((1/n, n\geq 2)\) on \(((0,1), d^u)\) This diverges to 0. It gets closer to 0, but the limit is not in our set, so it’s divergent. Example 2 \((n: n\geq 1)\) on \((\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) Can there exist some \(x \in \mathbb{R}\) such that for each \(\varepsilon > 0\) there exists \(N\) where \(|n-x| < \varepsilon\) for all \(n \geq N\)? No. For each \(\varepsilon\) there is some \(N\) with \(N > x + \varepsilon\). I also know for every \(n \geq N, |n - x| > \varepsilon\). 4.1.2 Def divergence Fix a real-valued sequence, \((x_n: n\geq 1)\) on \((X, d^u)\). \((x_n: n\geq 1)\) diverges to \(\infty\) if, for each \(k\in \mathbb{R}\), there exists \(N\) such that \(x_n>k\) for all \(n\geq N\). \((x_n: n\geq 1)\) diverges to \(-\infty\) if, for each \(k\in \mathbb{R}\), there exists \(N\) such that \(x_n<k\) for all \(n\geq N\). It’s convenient to write \(lim_{n\rightarrow \infty} x_n = \infty\). But this is an abuse of notation. Just remember that it doesn’t actually converge (bc you can’t converge outside your set). Case \((\mathbb{R}^m, d^{euc})\), \((x_n: n \in \mathbb{N})\) where \(x_n \in \mathbb{R}^m\). So \(x_n = (x_{n1}, x_{n2}, ..., x_{nm}) \in \mathbb{R}^m\) Result the sequence \((x_n: n\in\mathbb{N})\) converges to \(x^*\in\mathbb{R}^m\) iff for each \(i=1, ..., m\), \((x_{ni}: n\in \mathbb{N})\) converges to \(x_i^*\) in \((\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) Case \((X, d^{disc})\) the seq \((x_n: n \in \mathbb{N})\) converges to \(x\in X\) iff there exists some \(n\geq N\) such that \(x_n=x\) for all \(n\geq N\). Suppose \((x_n: n \in \mathbb{N})\) converges to \(x\in X\). Then, there exists some \(N\) such that \(1/2 > d^{disc}(x_n,x) \Rightarrow x_n=x\) for all \(n \geq N\) since \(d^{disc}(x_n,x) \in \{0,1\}\) (note that 1/2 is arbitrary–any value between 0 and 1 would have worked here.) 4.2 Subsequences seq \(= (1,6, 2, 9, 7, 5, 22, 1, ...)\) subsequence \(= (1,6, 9, 5, 22, ...)\) Def Fix a sequence \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\). Call \((y_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) a subsequence if there exists a function \(f: \mathbb{N}\rightarrow \mathbb{N}\) such that for each \(k\in \mathbb{N}\), 1. \(f(k+1) > f(k)\geq k\), 2. \(y_k=x_{f(k)}\) 4.2.1 Proposition 2 Let \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) be a sequence on \((X,d)\) that converges to \(x\). Then each subsequence of \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) also converges to \(x\). Proof Let \((y_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) be a subsequence of \((x_n: n\in\mathbb{N})\) associated with a function \(f: \mathbb{N}\rightarrow \mathbb{N}\). Since \((x_n: n\in\mathbb{N})\) converges to \(x\) for each \(\varepsilon>0\) there exists \(N\) such that \(d(x_n, x) < \varepsilon\), for all \(n \geq N\). There exists \(K\) such that \(f(K) \geq N\). Then it follows \(d(y_k, x) = d(x_{f(k)}, x) < \varepsilon\) for all \(k \geq K\) since \(f(k) \geq N\). Example 3 \(((-1)^n: n\geq 1)\). Pick two subsequences: \((-1, -1,..., ) \rightarrow\) converges to -1 \((1, 1,..., ) \rightarrow\) converges to 1 but a sequence cannot have two limits, so this sequence must diverge. Def Call a sequence \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) bounded if there exists \(y\in X\) and \(M>0\) such that \(d(x_n, y) \leq M\) for all \(n\in \mathbb{N}\). 4.2.2 Proposition 3 A convergent sequence is bounded. Proof Ommitted Caution: not every bounded sequence is convergent (example: \((-1)^n\) on \(X =\mathbb{R}, d^u\)) Def Let \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) be a real-valued sequence. The sequence is increasing if for each \(n \in \mathbb{N}, x_{n+1} \geq x_n\). Def The sequence is monotone if it is either increasing or decreasing. 4.2.3 Proposition 4 If \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) is a bounded, monotone sequence on \((\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) then it is convergent. Proof Fix \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) on \((\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) that is bounded, monotone sequence. Without loss of generality, assume it is increasing. Since bounded, the set \(\{x_n: n\in\mathbb{N}\}\) has to be bounded above (note: switching to set so we can use completeness axiom. apparently that may not apply to sequences, but it does to sets). By completeness axiom, if there is an upper bound, there must be a least upper bound, \(x\in \mathbb{R}\). Want to show: \(x=\lim x_n\) To show: Fix \(\varepsilon >0\), Find some \(N\) such that \(|x_n-x| < \varepsilon\), for all \(n \geq N\). Note: there exists \(N\) such that \(x_N > x-\varepsilon\), by Prop 1 of the real numbers notes. Since the seq is increasing \(x_n \geq x_N \geq x-\varepsilon\). Rearrange to get \(\varepsilon > x-x_n\). Since \(x\) is an upper bound (the supremum) we have \(x > x_n\) which means \(x-x_n = |x-x_n|\). So, we have \(\varepsilon > |x-x_n|\). QED. 4.2.4 Proposition 5 (aka squeeze thm): Let \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N}), (y_n: n\in \mathbb{N}), (z_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) be sequences on \((\mathbb{R}, d^u)\). If, for each \(n\), \(x_n \leq y_n \leq z_n\) and \(\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty} x_n = \lim_{n\rightarrow\infty} z_n = a\) then \(\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty} y_n = a\) Note: even if \(x_n\) and \(z_n\) crossed over each other at some points, I might still be able to use the squeeze thm by using subsequences to satisfy the fact that for each \(n\), \(x_n \leq y_n \leq z_n\) 4.2.5 Proposition 6: Let \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N}), (y_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) be sequences on \((\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) with \(\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty} x_n = x, \lim_{n\rightarrow\infty} y_n = y\). Then \(\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty} |x_n| = |x|\) \(\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty} x_n + y_n = x+y\) \(\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty} x_ny_n = xy\) If \(x_n \neq 0 \forall n\) and \(x\neq 0\), then \(\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty} y_n/x_n= y/x\) 4.2.6 Proposition 7: Bolzano-Weierstrauss Thm Every bounded sequence on \((\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) has a convergent subsequence. 4.2.7 Def “Cauchy” Call a sequence Cauchy if for each \(\varepsilon >0\) there exists \(N\) such that for all \(n, m \geq N, d(x_n, x_m) < \varepsilon\). 4.2.8 Proposition 8 Any convergent sequence is Cauchy. Proof Fix a sequence \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) with \(\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty}x_n=x\). So there exists \(g: (0,\infty) \rightarrow \mathbb{N}\) such that for each \(\varepsilon >0\) and \(n\geq g(\varepsilon), d(x_n,x) < \varepsilon\). To show it’s Cauchy, fix \(\varepsilon >0\) and choose \(N = g(\varepsilon/2)\). By triangle inequality I know \(d(x_n,x_m) \leq d(x_n, x) + d(x, x_m) < \varepsilon/2 + \varepsilon/2 = \varepsilon\). Remark: a seq can be Cauchy without converging. Consider example 1b: \((1/n, n\geq 2)\) on \(((0,1), d^u)\). This doesn’t converge (because 1 isn’t in the metric space), but it is cauchy. (i.e., for \(\varepsilon >0\) there exists \(N\) such that \(|1/n -1/m| < \varepsilon\) for all \(n, m \geq N\).) 4.2.9 Def of complete spaces A metric space is complete if every cauchy sequence converges. Examples of complete metric spaces: \((\mathbb{R}^n, d^{euc})\) \((X, d^{disc})\) for \(X\) finite \(((0,1), d^u)\) is NOT complete \((\mathbb{Q}, d^u)\) is NOT complete Remark: All cauchy sequences converge in complete spaces. 4.2.10 Proposition 9 Every cauchy sequence is bounded.

5 Class 4: 2023-08-17 5.1 Open/closed Sets Example 0: \((X,d)=(\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) 5.1.1 Def open ball \(E \subseteq X\) is an open ball around \(x \in X\) if there exists \(\varepsilon >0\) such that \(B(x, \varepsilon) = \{y \in X: d(x,y) < \varepsilon\} = E\) Example 1a \((X,d)=(\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) \(B(x,\varepsilon) = (x-\varepsilon, x + \varepsilon)\) Example 1b \((X,d)=([0,10], d^u)\) \(B(1/2, 3/4) = [0,5/4)\) (adding and subtracting epsilon of 3/4 from 1/2, but since -1/4 is outside our interval, just get as close as you can with 0, closed. still an open ball, just because it’s in [0,10]. but this would not be an open ball in \(\mathbb{R}\)) Example 2 \((X,d) = (\mathbb{R}^2, d^{euc})\) 5.1.2 Def interior point Fix \(E \subseteq X\). Say \(x\) is an interior point of \(E\) if there exists some \(\varepsilon >0\) such that \(B(x,\varepsilon) \subseteq E\). \(int(E) = \{x \in X: x \text{ is an interior point of } E\}\). If \(x \in B(x,\varepsilon)\subseteq E)\) then \(int(E) \subseteq E\). 5.1.3 Def open set A set \(E \subseteq X\) is an open set if for each \(x \in E, x \in int(E)\) \(E \subseteq int(E) \subseteq E\) - so an open set has \(E = int(E).\) Whether a set is an open set depends on \((X,d)\). In \((\mathbb{R}, d^u): [0,1)\) is NOT open because any \(B(0,\varepsilon)\) is not contained in [0,1). In \(([0,\infty),d^u): [0,1)\) IS OPEN because any \(B(0, \varepsilon)\) IS contained in [0,1) since nothing outside of \([0, \infty)\) exists in this metric space. Example 3 Let \(X\) be a finite or countable set. Let \(d\) be the discrete metric. In this case, each \(\{x\}\) would be an open set. Choose \(\varepsilon = 1/2\). Then \(B(x,1/2) = \{x\} \subseteq \{x\}\). What about \(E \subseteq X\)? This would also be an open set since for each \(x \in E, B(x, 1/2) = \{x\} \subseteq E\) Example 4 Fix \((X,d)=(\mathbb{R}, d^u)\). Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) finite or countable. Then \(E\) is not open. \(E\) is open iff \(E =int(E)\). Fix \(x\in E\) and suppose \(x \in int(E)\). Then, there exists \(\varepsilon >0\) such that \(B(x,\varepsilon) \subseteq E\). But this can’t be the case since \(B(x,\varepsilon)\) is UNCOUNTABLE while \(E\) is countable. So, clearly, \(x \notin int(E)\). Basically this means you’ll never have an open ball in a finite or countable set (at least not in \(\mathbb{R}\)). Example 5 Fix \((X,d)\). Fix \(E \in X\). Question: is \(int(E)\) an open set? Fix \(x\in int(E)\). Need to show, \(x \in int(int(E))\). Since \(x \in int(E)\) there exists \(\varepsilon >0\) such that \(B(x,\varepsilon) \subseteq E\). Fix \(y \in B(x, \varepsilon)\). Then there exists \(\varepsilon' >0\) such that \(B(y, \varepsilon') \subseteq B(x,\varepsilon) \subseteq E.\) So, \(y \in int(E)\). Yes! Since \(int(int(E))=int(E)\) 5.1.3.1 Proposition 1 Fix a metric space \((X,d)\) \(\emptyset, x\) are both open an arbitrary union of open sets is open a finite intersection of open sets is open this tells you that we have defined open sets so that they form a “topology” Example 6 \((\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) \(U_n = (-1/n, 1/n)\) for each \(n\in\mathbb{N}\). \(B(0,1/n)\), so open sets. \(\cap_{n \in \mathbb{N}}(-1/n,1/n) = \{0\}\) for some reason this is NOT open. apparently we showed before that singleton sets are not open in \(\mathbb{R}\) with the usual metric. 5.1.4 Def Closed Sets A set \(E\) is closed if \(X \setminus E\) is open. Example 7 Call \(E\) a closed ball around \(x\) if there exists some \(\varepsilon>0\) such that \(B[x, \varepsilon] = \{y \in X: d(x,y) \leq \varepsilon \} = E\) Claim: a closed ball is a closed set. To show: \(U = \{y \in X: d(x,y) > \varepsilon \}\). Fix \(z \in U\). To show: there exists an \(\varepsilon'\) such that \(B(z, \varepsilon') \subseteq U\). Take \(\varepsilon'=d(x,z)+\varepsilon\) and notice that since \(z\in U, \varepsilon' > 0\). Fix \(w \in B(z, \varepsilon')\) to show \(w \in u\) need \(d(x,w) > \varepsilon\). Notice \(d(x, w) + \varepsilon' >_{\text{by fact that } w \in B(??)} d(x,w) + d(w,z) \geq_{\text{by triangle}} d(x,z) \Rightarrow d(x,w) > d(x,z)-\varepsilon' = \varepsilon\) 5.1.4.1 Proposition 2 Fix a metric space \((X,d)\) \(\emptyset, X\) are closed sets the finite union of closed sets is closed an abitrary intersection of closed sets is closed \(C_1\) closed, then \(E_1 \cup E_2 \cup C_2 \cup E_3\) is open \(C_2\) closed, then \(E_1 \cup C_1 \cup E_2 \cup E_3\) is open Example 8 Countable union of closed sets may not be closed. \((\mathbb{R}, d^u), E_n[-1+ 1/n, 1 - 1/n]\) \(\cup_{n\in\mathbb{N}} E_n = (-1,1) \rightarrow\) not closed. 5.1.4.2 Proposition 3 A set \(E \subseteq X\) is closed if and only if, for any convergent sequence, \((x_n: n\geq 1)\) with \(x_n \in E, \lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} \in E\). Proof Step 1: Fix \(E\) closed. Fix \((x_n: n\geq1)\) convergent with each \(x_n\in E\). want to show: \(\lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} x_n \in E\) Suppose for contradiction that \(\lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} x_n = x^* \in X\setminus E\). We will show there is \(n\) with \(x_n \in X \setminus E\) Notice: \(X \setminus E\) is open (since \(E\) is closed.) So, there exists \(\varepsilon > 0\) such that \(B(x^*, \varepsilon) \subseteq X\setminus E\) By definition of the limit, since \(\lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} x_n = x^*\) there exists some \(N\) such that for all \(n \geq N, d(x_n, x^*) < \varepsilon\). This implies that for all \(n\geq N, x_n \in B(x^*, \varepsilon) \subseteq X \setminus E\). But this is a contradiction since it was given that each \(x_n\in E\) Step 2: Suppose that for each convergent sequence \((x_n: n\geq 1)\) with each \(x_n \in E, \lim_{n\rightarrow \infty} x_n \in E\) To show: \(E\) is closed (or \(X\setminus E\) is open) Suppose \(X \setminus E\) is not open. So there exists \(x \in X \setminus E\) such that for each \(n \in \mathbb{N}, B(x, 1/n) \cap E \neq \emptyset\) Choose \(x_n \in B(x, 1/n) \cap E\) for each \(n\in\mathbb{N}\). Have a sequence \((x_n: n\geq 1)\) in \(E\). \(\lim_{n\rightarrow \infty} x_n = x \in X\setminus E\). THis contradicts the fact that contains limits of any sequence in \(E\).
6 Class 5: 2023-08-18 6.1 Open/closed Sets, cont 6.2 Compactness 6.3 Continuity 6.4 Other notions of continuity

6 Class 5: 2023-08-18 6.1 Open/closed Sets, cont 6.1.0.1 Def “adherent” Call \(x\in X\) an adherent point of \(E\), if, for each \(\varepsilon >0, B(x, \varepsilon) \cap E 
eq \emptyset\). 6.1.0.2 Def “closure” The closure of \(E\) is \(cl(E) = \{x \in X: x 	ext{ is an adherent point of } E\}\). 6.1.0.3 Def “boundary” The boundary of \(E\) is \(cl(E) \setminus int(E)\) 6.1.0.4 Proposition 4 Fix \(E \subseteq X\) the \(int(E)\) is the largest open set contained in \(E\). the \(cl(E)\) is the smallest closed set that contains \(E\). 6.2 Compactness 6.2.1 Def “open cover” Fix \(E \subseteq X\). Call \(\{F_i: i \in I\}\) a cover of \(E\) if each \(F_i \subseteq X\) and \(E \subseteq \cup_{i \in I} F_i\). Call \(\{F_i: i \in I\}\) an open cover of \(E\) if it is a cover and each \(F_i\) is open. 6.2.2 Def “Compact” Call \(E \subseteq X\) compact if every open cover admits a finite subcover. That is, if \(\{F_i: i \in I\}\) is an open cover of \(E\) then there exists finite \(J \subseteq I\) such that \(\{F_i: i \in J\}\) is also a cover of \(E\). Example 9 Let \((X,d) = (\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) Let \(E = (0,1)\). \(\{\mathbb{R}\}\) is an open cover of \(E\) (trivial example.) \(\{(1/n,1): n \in \mathbb{N}\}\) is an open cover of \(E=(0,1)\). (note that \(\cup_{n\in\mathbb{N}}(1/n,1)=(0,1)\)) For any \(J \subseteq \mathbb{N}\) finite, \(\cup_{n\in J} (1/n, 1) = (1/m, 1)\) where \(m\) is the max number in \(J\). 6.2.2.1 Def “bounded set” Call \(E \subseteq X\) bounded if there exists \(x \in X\) and \(r>0\) such that \(E \subseteq B(x,r)\) 6.2.2.2 Proposition 5 If \(E\) is compact then \(E\) is both closed and bounded. Example: \((X, d) = (\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) is closed, but not bounded so it’s not compact. (remember, \(\mathbb{R}\) is both closed and open) Example 10a Will show \(E\) can be close and bounded but need not be compact. Let \(X = \{1/n, n\in\mathbb{N}\}\), \(d\) = discrete metric. Then \(E=X\) is closed. It’s also bounded: pick any \(x \in X\) and get \(E \subseteq B(x,2)\). But, it doesn’t admit a finite subcover: \(\{\{1/n\}: n \in \mathbb{N}\} ightarrow\) covers \(E\) and is open. Example 10b \((X,d) = (\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) \(E = \{1/n: n \in \mathbb{N}\}\) then a cover is \(\{1/n: n \in \mathbb{N}\}\) but this is not open. all singleton sets (and even all finite sets) are NOT open for some reason????????? 6.2.2.3 Heine-Borel Theorem Let \((X,d)=(\mathbb{R}^n, d^{euc})\). A set \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n\) is compact \(\iff\) it is closed and bounded. Note that this an iff relationship only if you are in \(\mathbb{R}^n\). Not necessarily true outside of \(\mathbb{R}^n\) 6.2.2.4 Proposition 6 If \(E \subseteq F \subseteq X\) where \(E\) is closed and \(F\) is compact, then \(E\) is compact. 6.2.2.5 Proposition 7 If \((X,d)\) is a compact metric space, then \(X\) is complete. reminder: complete = every cauchy sequence converges 6.3 Continuity Fix \((X, d_x)\) and \((Y,d_y)\). Look at \(f: X ightarrow Y\). 6.3.0.1 Def “continuous at” Say a function \(f: X ightarrow Y\) is continuous at \(x\) if for each \(\varepsilon > 0\) there exists \(\delta >0\) such that \(d_x(x,x') < \delta\) implies \(d_y(f(x),f(x')) < \varepsilon\) for each \(\delta > 0\) there is an \(x'\) with \(d_x(x,x') < \delta\) but \(f(x') > J\) and so \(d_y(f(x),f(x')) > \varepsilon\) \(\varepsilon = d_y(f(x), J)\) 6.3.0.2 Proposition 1 A function \(f: X ightarrow Y\) is continuous at \(x\in X \iff\) for each sequence \((x_n: n \geq 1)\) on \((X, d_x)\) with \(lim_{nightarrow \infty} x_n = x \in X, lim_{nightarrow \infty} f(x_n) = f(x) \in Y\) 6.3.0.3 Def “continuous function” A function \(f: X ightarrow Y\) is continuous if it is continuous at each \(x\in X\). 6.3.0.4 Proposition 2 A function \(f: Xightarrow X\) is continuous if and only if, for each open set, \(U \subseteq Y, f^{-1}(U) \subseteq X\) is open. \(x \in f^{-1}(u)\) is not an interior point because for each \(B(x, \varepsilon)\) there is discontinuity within the ball. Example 1 Simple examples of continuous functions: \((X, d_x) = (Y,d_y)\), take \(f: Xightarrow Y\) to be the identity function, \(f(x)=x\in Y = X\). Fix \(U \subseteq Y = X\) open in \((Y, d_y) \Rightarrow f^{-1}(U)=U\) open in \((X,d_x)=(Y,d_y)\) start with \(\{x\}\) in \((Y,d_y) = (\mathbb{R}, d^{disc})\) then \(f^{-1}(\{x\}) = \{x\}\) not open in \((\mathbb{R}, d^{u})\) singleton sets are open in discrete metric, but not in Euclidean metric! Constant function: \(f: Xightarrow Y\) there exists \(y^* \in Y\) such that \(f(x)=y^*\) for all \(x\in X\). Example 2 \((X, d_x)\) where \(X\) is finite. Any \(f: X ightarrow Y\) is continuous. key: since \(X\) is finite, \(f^{-1}(U)\) must be open since any subset of a finite set is open. Example 3 \(X=Y=\mathbb{R}, d_x=d_y=d^u\). \(f(x)=mx+b\) for \(m 
eq 0\). to show \(f\) is continuous, fix \(\varepsilon >0\) and some \(\delta >0\) such that \(|x-x'|< \delta\) then \(|mx+b - (mx' + b)| = |m||x-x'| < \varepsilon\). Take \(\delta = \varepsilon / |m|\) and we can show this. 6.3.0.5 Proposition 3 Fix \(f: X ightarrow \mathbb{R}\) and \(g: X ightarrow \mathbb{R}\) that are both continuous (on \((x, d_x), (\mathbb{R}, d^u)\)) \(f+g\) is continuous (\(x ightarrow f(x) + g(x)\)) \(f * g\) is continuous (\(x ightarrow f(x) * g(x)\)) \(f/g\) is continuous (if, for each \(x\in X, g(x)
eq 0\)) (\(x ightarrow f(x) / g(x)\)) \(\max\{f,g\}\) is continuous (\(x ightarrow \max\{f(x), g(x)\}\)) \(\min\{f,g\}\) is continuous (\(x ightarrow \min\{f(x), g(x)\}\)) \(|f|\) is continuous (\(xightarrow |f(x)|\)) so, using prop 3 on example 3 could simplify things. we’d just say, “well, we know any constant (\(m\) and \(b\)) function is continuous, and we know the identity function (\(f(x) = x\)) is continuous, and we know that the product of two continuous functions is continuous (number 2 in prop 3) and the sum of two continuous function (\(mx\) and \(b\)) is continuous, so \(f(x) = mx + b\) is continuous.” 6.4 Other notions of continuity 6.4.0.1 Def “lower semi-continuous” Fix a function \(f: Xightarrow \mathbb{R}\). \(f\) is lower semi-continuous if, for each \(c \in \mathbb{R}, f^{-1}(c,\infty)\) is open \(f\) is upper semi-continuous if, for each \(c \in \mathbb{R}, f^{-1}(-\infty, c)\) is open It’s immediate that if \(f\) is continuous, then it’s both upper and lower semi-continuous. It’s less obvious (though we will see this is, indeed, true later) that if \(f\) if upper and lower semi-continuous that \(f\) is continuous. Example 4b \(f: \mathbb{R} ightarrow \mathbb{R}\) such that \[f(x) =  egin{cases} 0 	ext{ if } x \leq 0 \ 1 	ext{ if } x>0 \end{cases}\] Choose \(c = 1/2\), an arbitrary point between 0 and 1. Checking upper semi-continuity: \[f^{-1}(-\infty, 1/2) = (-\infty, 0]\] this is not open, so we fail upper semi-continuity. so, \(f\) is not continuous. Checking lower semi-continuity: \[f^{-1}(c,\infty) =  egin{cases} \emptyset 	ext{ if } c \geq 1 \ (0,\infty) 	ext{ if } 0 \leq c < 1 \ \mathbb{R} 	ext{ if } c < 0 \end{cases}\] These are all open sets, so \(f\) is lower semi-continuous. Now, changing the function: \[g(x) =  egin{cases} 0 	ext{ if } x<0 \ 1 	ext{ if } x \geq 0 \end{cases}\] Checking upper semi-continuity: \[g^{-1}((-\infty, c)) = \{x\in \mathbb{R}: f(x) < c\}\] \[g^{-1}((-\infty, c)) =  egin{cases} \emptyset 	ext{ if } c \leq 0 \ (\infty, 0) 	ext{ if } 0 < c \leq 1 \ \mathbb{R} 	ext{ if } c > 1 \end{cases}\] Checking lower semi-continuity: \[g^{-1}((1/2, \infty)) = [0,\infty)\] which is NOT open. 6.4.0.2 Proposition 4 A function \(f: X ightarrow \mathbb{R}\) is continuous if and only if it is both upper and lower semi-continuous. coming soon: strengthening of continuity (uniform continuity will imply continuity.)
